title: ASX to rise as oil swings on war; fuel costs hit business
content:
Inflation expectations hit record high

KMD Brands narrows first-half loss, refinances debt

Shaver Shop appoints Bendall to board

Collins Foods to offload majority of Taco Bell stores

West African Resources flags record output in 2026

Fuel costs overtake all other pressures for SMEs: MYOB

Australian shares are set to open higher, with ASX 200 futures closing up 11 points, or 0.1 per cent, to 8500.

Wall Street closed lower after paring earlier gains, with the S&P 500 falling 0.4 per cent, as investors weighed easing rate concerns against ongoing geopolitical risks.

US bond yields declined after Federal Reserve chair Jerome Powell dampened expectations of further rate rises, with the 10-year yield falling 8 basis points to 4.35 per cent.

Oil prices were volatile, with Brent crude swinging between $US112 and $US116 a barrel, as a Group of Seven pledge to support energy markets offset concerns about an escalation in the Middle East.

“From our perspective, the equity market is priced for the ‘Ongoing Constraints’ scenario laid out by our commodity strategists where crude ends the second quarter at $US110 a barrel before falling to $US80,” Morgan Stanley’s US equities team said.

“In terms of our targets, we continue to see 7800 on the S&P 500 as achievable assuming a recession is avoided, which remains our baseline. The risk is that it takes slightly longer to reach that target if oil prices remain elevated/around current levels in the near-term.

“However, on a six- to 12-month basis, the risk-reward now looks more attractive than at the beginning of the year given the 15 per cent-plus decline in multiples which we think is more than the actual hit to forward earnings.”

ASX 200 futures are pointing up 11 points or 0.1 per cent to 8500.All US prices near 4.15pm New York time.

The Reserve Bank of Australia is set to release minutes from its latest policy meeting at 11.30am. February private sector credit for February is also due to be released Tuesday.

China is scheduled to release March manufacturing and non-manufacturing PMI data at 12.30pm.

TD Securities: “We expect both PMIs to stay in contraction (greater than 50), with manufacturing at 49.5 (consensus: 50.2) and non-manufacturing at 49.8 (consensus: 50).

“The start of the US-Iran conflict in early March may keep manufacturing sentiment depressed as firms grapple with a surge in input prices, largely from the jump in oil prices. Likewise for non-manufacturing, construction costs are likely to climb which is another blow to developers that are barely afloat due to the weakness in the property market.”

Fuel excise cut hits budget bottom line | The introduction of temporary cuts to fuel taxes suggests reform will take a back seat to cost-of-living relief in the upcoming May budget, economists warn.

‘Don’t mention the war’: Trick is to make Trump think he’s won | Anthony Albanese doesn’t believe Donald Trump has effected regime change in Iran, but nor does he want the US president to think he hasn’t.

Navigator Global bets $145m on Canadian AI venture capital investor | The ASX-listed asset manager says the deal to buy a stake in Georgian will give shareholders exposure to artificial intelligence at a time of major disruption.

Super funds at risk as war triggers ‘overcrowding’ of the ASX | Investors are piling into the biggest companies at the fastest pace since the GFC, creating concentration risk for passive investors tracking the ASX 200.

Pipeline giant backs LNG imports despite war-driven price surge | Jemena’s investment in gas pipeline capacity to support LNG imports comes amid warnings from some producers about becoming over-reliant on external supplies.
